patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting manager. Start with: “I’ve had this burning, electric pain deep in my pelvis and groin, especially when I sit.” Volunteer: burning/electric pain between the legs, tingling/pins-and-needles in groin/perineum, worse the longer you sit, better standing/lying down, and “like sitting on a small rock/rolled-up sock.” If asked, add: usually worse on one side, can spread to inner thigh; flares with biking, long drives, hard chairs; toilet seat is much better; can wake you after you fall asleep; occasional numbness; sharp zaps with cough/strain; urinary urgency; constipation/feeling unable to relax during flares. Do not mention unless asked directly: you perch/lean to one side, avoid tight clothes, stopped cycling/workouts, clench pelvic muscles, pain builds through day and peaks late afternoon/evening. Be reluctant/embarrassed about sex: painful sex, burning after, pain with orgasm; worry something is wrong with your genitals; feel down/irritable. Share misconceptions: you suspected UTI/hemorrhoids/sciatica; tests/creams didn’t help. Express cost/work concerns; assume PT is expensive and surgery is the only worthwhile option. If clinician shows empathy, soften, admit fear/embarrassment, and become more open.

acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with neuropathic features

No objective sensory loss on exam (often normal neuro exam; Nantes criteria feature)

Optional supportive evidence: symptom improvement after diagnostic pudendal nerve block

OR pregabalin 25-50 mg PO at bedtime, titrate to 50-75 mg PO twice daily as tolerated
Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily for 7 days then 60 mg PO daily if tolerated (avoid if significant liver disease or heavy alcohol use)

non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy focused on relaxation/down-training (avoid strengthening if hypertonic pelvic floor suspected)

Avoid triggers (cycling/rowing, straining/constipation); bowel regimen as needed (fiber, hydration)
Consider image-guided diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block; consider referral to pain medicine/PM&R
If refractory: consider neuromodulation or surgical decompression only after specialist evaluation and confirmation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for symptom response/tolerability; earlier (within 1 week) if adverse effects or worsening symptoms

must_include: Condition involves irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve causing neuropathic perineal symptoms (paresthesia/pain)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for pudendal neuralgia (pain/paresthesia distribution, sitting-related worsening, triggers such as cycling, duration, functional impact) and screen for red flags and alternative diagnoses.

Clarify prior evaluations/treatments, current medication list (including OTC/supplements), pregnancy status if relevant, renal/hepatic disease, substance/alcohol use, and driving/occupation safety considerations.

If clinically indicated by history (systemic symptoms, infection risk, inflammatory signs), order targeted tests to exclude alternative causes; otherwise document that labs are not routinely required for classic presentations.
Review any ordered results and ensure no evidence of alternative urgent pathology before committing to diagnosis/treatment plan.
State working diagnosis (pudendal neuralgia) based on clinical pattern and exclusion of red flags; note differential and rationale.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, including mechanism (irritation/entrapment), clinical nature of diagnosis, and urgent red flags requiring reassessment.
Provide stepwise treatment explanation: activity modification, pelvic floor PT, neuropathic pain options, and procedural escalation if refractory.
Critical safety check prior to any prescribing; confirm allergies and reaction types relevant to neuropathic agents and topical anesthetics.
Critical safety check prior to prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds (e.g., CNS depressants with gabapentinoids, serotonergic agents with duloxetine).

Gather contraindication-relevant details if not already obtained (e.g., severe renal impairment for gabapentin/pregabalin dosing, uncontrolled hypertension for duloxetine, glaucoma/urinary retention history if considering other agents).

Initiate an evidence-based first-line neuropathic pain medication with gradual titration and provide clear instructions; avoid opioids as routine first-line; consider topical lidocaine as adjunct if appropriate.

Counsel on medication expectations (time to benefit, titration, do not stop abruptly), common adverse effects/precautions (sedation/dizziness, nausea/BP changes), alcohol moderation, and driving/operating machinery warnings.

Lifestyle education: sitting strategies (breaks, standing intervals, offloading cushion), avoid cycling/perineal pressure during flare, ergonomic adjustments, constipation/straining avoidance and pelvic floor relaxation techniques.
Arrange follow-up to assess response/tolerability and adjust therapy; provide clear return precautions for red flags and significant side effects.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: constant urination including at night, dry mouth/always thirsty, carrying a large water bottle for weeks, feeling wiped out and sometimes lightheaded.

If asked for details, say you wake 3–5 times nightly to pee; urine is very clear; you drink several large bottles/day; if you can’t drink you get headache and feel shaky; a few pounds of unintentional weight loss; occasional blurry vision when very thirsty; no burning or pain with urination.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: irritability/can’t focus without water, dry skin/lips, constipation, heart racing when standing, avoiding long drives/movies.

Resist sharing unless rapport is strong: a couple accidents, lower sex drive, skipping social plans.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s diabetes; started a “detox” to drink as much water as possible; more coffee/energy drinks; wondered about UTI.

If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share financial worries about labs, visits, missing work, and costs of drinks/water.

medications: Meclocycline 300 mg PO twice daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg PO daily
confounders: High daily caffeine intake (energy drinks) contributing to diuresis
Use of tetracycline-class drug (meclocycline) which can induce nephrogenic DI-like physiology and complicate interpretation
Primary polydipsia in differential due to stress/anxiety and habitual high water intake

required_evidence: Polyuria with elevated 24-hour urine volume (typically >3 L/day in adults) and dilute urine (low urine osmolality and low specific gravity)
High-normal or elevated serum sodium and elevated serum osmolality in the absence of hyperglycemia
Low spot urine osmolality despite increased plasma osmolality (inappropriately dilute urine)
Renal function not severely impaired (BUN/creatinine not explaining concentrating defect)
Clinical context consistent with impaired ADH production/release (central DI) rather than osmotic diuresis

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: High-normal to elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake is insufficient)
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 mOsm/kg in complete DI)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (often >3 L/day; can be markedly higher)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (fasting): Normal (helps exclude osmotic diuresis from diabetes mellitus)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated if dehydrated; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal unless concurrent kidney disease/dehydration

medications: Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO twice daily (titrate to lowest effective dose)
OR Desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal once daily to twice daily (titrate based on urine output and serum sodium)
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water; drink to thirst and avoid intentional fluid restriction
Monitor daily weight and urine volume when initiating/titrating therapy
Avoid excess free-water intake once desmopressin started to reduce hyponatremia risk
Review and discontinue/avoid ineffective or inappropriate agents for central DI (e.g., meclocycline is not appropriate for central DI and can cause nephrotoxicity)
Evaluate for underlying cause of central DI (e.g., pituitary/hypothalamic pathology) as part of ongoing care
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and symptoms within 3–7 days after starting or changing desmopressin; then every 1–3 months until stable, and periodically thereafter.

must_include: Condition causes inability to concentrate urine due to insufficient ADH (vasopressin) production/release
Explains excessive urination and thirst and can lead to dehydration and high sodium if not matched with water intake
Different from diabetes mellitus; glucose is not the cause
Desmopressin replaces the missing ADH and reduces urine volume and thirst
Dose is individualized; goal is symptom control with safe sodium levels
Main serious risk is hyponatremia/water intoxication if taking desmopressin and drinking excessive fluids
Warning signs of hyponatremia: headache, nausea, confusion, severe fatigue, seizures—seek urgent care
Warning signs of dehydration/hypernatremia: extreme thirst, dizziness, weakness, confusion—seek care
Plan for sick days/vomiting/limited access to water and when to hold or adjust desmopressin per clinician guidance
Drink to thirst; avoid both fluid restriction and compulsive overdrinking once on desmopressin
Keep a log of urine output, thirst, and daily weight during titration
Importance of follow-up labs (serum sodium) after starting/changing therapy and periodically

Order initial evaluation to support/confirm DI and assess safety: serum sodium, serum osmolality, glucose, BUN/Cr, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, and urine volume (24h if feasible).
Interpret labs for DI pattern (inappropriately dilute urine with elevated/normal-high serum osmolality and/or hypernatremia) and exclude diabetes mellitus (hyperglycemia).
Make/confirm diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus (vs nephrogenic DI/primary polydipsia) based on history and results; document differential and rationale.
Critical safety check before prescribing desmopressin or any adjunct medications.
Critical safety check for drugs that increase hyponatremia risk with desmopressin (e.g., SSRIs/SNRIs, thiazides, carbamazepine, NSAIDs) or other clinically relevant interactions.
Critical safety check before prescribing: ensure no current hyponatremia, significant fluid overload states, or inability to adhere to fluid guidance/monitoring; confirm appropriate indication.
Initiate desmopressin with individualized dosing strategy and monitoring plan (symptom control while maintaining safe serum sodium).
Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment, risks (hyponatremia), warning signs, sick-day plan, and lifestyle/monitoring instructions.
Arrange timely follow-up with repeat serum sodium after initiation/dose change and periodic monitoring; provide escalation instructions.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial hypercholesterolemia (autosomal dominant) with premature coronary artery disease risk
acceptable_alternatives: Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) due to genetic dyslipidemia

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Elevated (often markedly high, e.g., >300 mg/dL) consistent with familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype
expected_lab_results.LDL cholesterol: Markedly elevated (often >190 mg/dL; can be >250 mg/dL in more severe phenotypes)
expected_lab_results.HDL cholesterol: Variable; may be normal or low (not diagnostic)

non_pharmacological: Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean/DASH; reduce saturated and trans fats; increase soluble fiber)

Weight management and blood pressure optimization

Address other risk factors: diabetes prevention/management based on A1c, sleep, stress, alcohol moderation

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male IT support specialist (college educated). You are cooperative and communicate clearly, with a mildly worried tone.

At the start, volunteer: leg weakness gradually worsening over ~2 years with more tripping; stairs and rising from low chairs are harder; hands feel weaker (dropping things, trouble opening jars); you fatigue faster on longer walks.

If asked, add: toe-catching causing near-falls/falls; shoulder weakness overhead/washing hair; tight/crampy muscles after activity; visible muscle shrinking in lower legs/forearms; occasional shortness of breath when lying flat or very tired.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: using arms to push off thighs to stand; weak/tired voice by day’s end; occasional morning headaches and unrefreshing sleep.

Resist sharing choking/coughing with thin liquids; if directly asked about swallowing, admit a couple episodes and say it’s embarrassing so you ignored it.

Express misconceptions: occasional tingling makes you suspect a nerve problem; you worry about motor neuron disease because it started in feet/hands.

Bring up cost concerns: insurance coverage for genetics/specialists; think PT only helps with frequent clinic visits.

If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open.

medications: Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
Metoprolol succinate 25 mg PO daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/actuation 2 puffs inhaled q4-6h PRN wheeze
allergies: Succinylcholine (history of severe hyperkalemia and rhabdomyolysis after anesthesia; avoid depolarizing neuromuscular blockers)
confounders: Current statin exposure avoided due to prior myalgias; history of intermittent statin use could mimic myopathic symptoms
Recent increase in exercise/physical therapy can transiently elevate CK and worsen myalgias
Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation with mild dyspnea can be misattributed to respiratory muscle weakness
Mild asthma with episodic shortness of breath complicates assessment of neuromuscular respiratory involvement
Borderline elevated NT-proBNP raises concern for cardiomyopathy (possible in myofibrillar myopathy) versus unrelated cardiac disease

required_evidence: Progressive muscle weakness pattern consistent with myopathy (e.g., distal or scapuloperoneal involvement) with myopathic features on exam
Myopathic pattern on EMG (if available) and/or muscle MRI supportive of myopathy
Muscle biopsy showing myofibrillar disorganization with protein aggregates (e.g., desmin-positive inclusions) and Z-disc pathology

Genetic testing identifying a pathogenic variant in a myofibrillar myopathy-associated gene (e.g., DES, FLNC, MYOT, LDB3/ZASP, BAG3, CRYAB) OR biopsy strongly supportive when genetics pending/negative
Exclusion of primary neurogenic process (no clear upper motor neuron signs; neuropathy/neuronopathy not the primary driver)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated; may be moderately elevated during exertion or episodes of rhabdomyolysis
expected_lab_results.Aldolase: Normal to mildly elevated, paralleling CK when elevated
expected_lab_results.AST: May be mildly elevated due to muscle source (especially if CK elevated)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mildly elevated; if elevated, interpret alongside CK to assess muscle contribution
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I: Typically normal; elevation suggests possible cardiac involvement and warrants cardiology evaluation
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Typically normal; elevation suggests cardiomyopathy/heart failure risk in some myofibrillar myopathies
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal unless renal injury from rhabdomyolysis
expected_lab_results.eGFR: Usually normal unless acute kidney injury from rhabdomyolysis

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease)
IV 0.9% sodium chloride bolus 1000 mL IV once then 150-250 mL/hour IV continuous if rhabdomyolysis suspected/confirmed, titrate to urine output and clinical status
non_pharmacological: Avoid succinylcholine and other depolarizing neuromuscular blockers; ensure allergy/adverse reaction is prominently documented and anesthesia alerted
Referral to neuromuscular specialist for confirmation (biopsy/genetics review) and longitudinal care
Baseline and periodic cardiac screening (ECG and echocardiogram; consider Holter) due to potential cardiomyopathy/arrhythmia in some myofibrillar myopathies
Physical therapy: individualized low-to-moderate intensity strengthening and stretching; avoid overexertion that provokes rhabdomyolysis
Occupational therapy for adaptive equipment and energy conservation
Respiratory assessment (spirometry/FVC) if symptoms suggest hypoventilation or bulbar involvement; consider sleep study if nocturnal symptoms
Genetic counseling and family screening as appropriate once genotype identified
Rhabdomyolysis precautions: maintain hydration during illness/exertion; seek care for dark urine, severe myalgias, weakness, or reduced urine output
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular clinic follow-up in 4-8 weeks (earlier within 1 week if CK markedly elevated, renal function abnormal, or new cardiac/respiratory symptoms)

must_include: Myofibrillar myopathy is a genetic/structural muscle disease affecting muscle fibers (not primarily a nerve disease)
Course is typically slowly progressive and varies by subtype
Some subtypes can involve the heart and/or breathing muscles, so screening is important
No curative medication; management focuses on function, symptom control, and monitoring for complications
Role of PT/OT and safe exercise plan; avoid overexertion that can trigger muscle breakdown
Plan for cardiac (and respiratory if indicated) surveillance
Explicitly confirm history of severe hyperkalemia/rhabdomyolysis with succinylcholine and state it must be avoided in future anesthesia
Discuss emergency warning signs: dark urine, severe muscle pain/weakness, shortness of breath, palpitations/syncope
Discuss family implications and option for genetic counseling/testing
Hydration strategies, especially during illness or heat/exertion
Avoid triggers for rhabdomyolysis (extreme exertion, dehydration); gradual conditioning under guidance
Medication/surgery planning: inform all clinicians/anesthesiologists of succinylcholine reaction and carry medical alert information

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history: symptom pattern (proximal/distal weakness, onset, progression), functional limitations, exercise intolerance/cramps, myoglobinuria/rhabdomyolysis history, family history, cardiac/respiratory symptoms, anesthesia history (including succinylcholine reaction), meds/supplements, and goals.
Screen for red flags needing urgent escalation: dark urine, severe muscle pain/rapid weakness, dyspnea/orthopnea, palpitations/syncope, chest pain.

Order baseline labs commonly used to assess muscle injury/complications and safety for activity planning (e.g., CK, CMP including potassium/creatinine, AST/ALT, urine analysis for heme/myoglobin) and consider thyroid/Vit D if part of local protocol.

Critical safety check before any ordering that could include medications/contrast/anesthesia-related agents; confirm and document succinylcholine-associated severe hyperkalemia/rhabdomyolysis and other allergies.
If considering any new medication for symptoms (e.g., pain/spasm), check interactions with current meds/supplements and renal/hepatic status.
Review lab results to identify active rhabdomyolysis, kidney injury, electrolyte abnormalities, or hepatic enzyme elevation that would change counseling/urgency.
Confirm/communicate myofibrillar myopathy diagnosis (or working diagnosis) and outline subtype variability and need for cardiac/respiratory screening.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment/monitoring plan, succinylcholine avoidance and emergency signs, family/genetic counseling, lifestyle/hydration and safe exercise guidance.
Order/arrange appropriate surveillance testing (typically cardiac screening such as ECG and echocardiogram; consider pulmonary function testing if symptoms or subtype risk).

Critical contraindication check before ordering tests/therapies that may be unsafe given comorbidities (e.g., exercise intensity plans, certain meds, contrast if applicable); ensure ordering aligns with renal function, pregnancy status if relevant, and prior reactions.
Arrange follow-up to review results, coordinate PT/OT, genetics, cardiology/pulmonology as indicated, and provide ongoing monitoring plan.
Close encounter with clear next steps, return precautions, and documentation of critical anesthesia precaution.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) due to LACC1 deficiency (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic autoinflammatory disease with chronic inflammatory arthritis (LACC1-associated)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (≥6 weeks) with objective joint swelling and/or limitation with pain
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP), often with hyperferritinemia during flares
CBC evidence of inflammation (anemia of chronic disease and/or thrombocytosis; leukocytosis possible)
Autoantibodies typically negative or low-titer (RF negative; ANA may be negative or nonspecific)
Exclusion of septic arthritis/osteomyelitis and malignancy based on clinical course and labs
Known genetic diagnosis or strong suspicion of LACC1 defect in appropriate phenotype (early onset, refractory, familial/consanguinity)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low-normal to low (anemia of chronic disease possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Elevated (reactive thrombocytosis common in active disease)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated; may be markedly elevated during systemic inflammation/flares
expected_lab_results.ANA (IFA): Often negative; if positive, typically low titer and nonspecific
expected_lab_results.Rheumatoid factor: Negative (most pediatric cases; RF-positive is less likely)

medications: Prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day PO daily (short course for flare control; taper over 2-4 weeks to lowest effective dose)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral/coordination with pediatric rheumatology for steroid-sparing therapy planning (e.g., DMARD/biologic selection given LACC1-associated disease)
Physical therapy and daily range-of-motion exercises to prevent contractures and maintain function
Occupational therapy and school/activity accommodations as needed
Vaccination review: avoid live vaccines while on high-dose systemic steroids; update inactivated vaccines
Bone health: ensure adequate calcium/vitamin D intake; consider bone protection strategy if prolonged steroids anticipated
Screen for systemic features and complications (uveitis risk assessment; ophthalmology referral if indicated by JIA subtype/ANA status or symptoms)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for response/toxicity and to initiate/confirm steroid-sparing regimen with pediatric rheumatology; sooner (24-48 hours) if worsening pain, fever, inability to bear weight, or concern for infection

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis beginning in childhood; LACC1 is a genetic cause that can drive ongoing inflammation
Explain what the labs mean (ESR/CRP reflect inflammation; CBC changes can occur with inflammation)

Medication adherence and not stopping steroids abruptly without a taper plan

Maintain activity within pain limits; avoid prolonged immobilization; use PT/OT strategies

Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene; prompt evaluation for significant infections; vaccine guidance)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I keep tripping over my feet, and my ankles feel weak.” Early in the visit, volunteer that your toes feel numb, it’s been going on for years and slowly worsening, and you’ve always had high arches with shoes wearing out oddly.

If asked about walking, mention you can’t heel-walk well, sometimes drag your toes, and your feet “slap.” If asked about appearance, say your calves look thinner and your toes are starting to curl with recurring calluses; you’ve switched to wider shoes. If asked about sensation or pain, report reduced feeling for small cuts/hot bath water and occasional burning/tingling at night. If asked about balance, say it’s worse in the dark/uneven ground. If asked about hands, describe fatigue with buttons/jars when stressed or tired. If asked about family history, say several relatives have “weak ankles/foot problems.”

Do not volunteer embarrassment or job fears unless the clinician is empathetic; then admit you’re scared of falling and being judged, and worried about work. Express misconceptions: possible pinched back nerve, diabetes, or vitamin deficiency. If empathy is offered, you open up and ask about costs (braces/PT) and concerns about genetic diagnosis affecting insurance/employment.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (feet/legs > hands), often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood
Sensory loss in a length-dependent pattern (reduced vibration/proprioception and/or pinprick in feet)
Decreased or absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (pes cavus/high arches, hammer toes) and/or recurrent ankle sprains/foot drop with steppage gait
Family history of similar neuropathy or foot deformities (autosomal dominant pattern common, but can be sporadic)
Supportive testing when available: nerve conduction studies showing demyelinating (uniformly slowed) or axonal neuropathy; genetic testing confirming a pathogenic variant

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from CMT itself)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected from CMT itself)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Typically normal (used to help exclude diabetic neuropathy; elevated would suggest an alternative/complicating diagnosis)

medications: Gabapentin 100-300 mg PO at bedtime, then titrate to 300 mg PO three times daily as tolerated for neuropathic pain (if present)
Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily, increase to 60 mg PO daily as tolerated for neuropathic pain (alternative/adjunct if pain present)
non_pharmacological: Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, and gait training
Occupational therapy for hand weakness, adaptive devices, and energy conservation strategies
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop and ankle instability; supportive footwear/orthotics for pes cavus
Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformities; consider surgical correction if severe or refractory to bracing
Genetic counseling and offer genetic testing; discuss family planning implications
Avoid or minimize exposure to medications known to worsen peripheral neuropathy (e.g., vincristine; caution with other neurotoxic agents)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, and pain control in 4-6 weeks after initiating supportive measures/neuropathic pain therapy; then every 6-12 months (earlier if rapid change, frequent falls, or new neurologic deficits)

must_include: CMT is a hereditary peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves
Typical course is slowly progressive; symptoms often start in feet/legs and can involve hands later
Not contagious; not caused by infection
Confirmatory testing options (nerve conduction studies and genetic testing) and what results mean
Red flags warranting urgent reassessment (rapid progression, new bowel/bladder issues, significant asymmetry, severe new pain)
No curative therapy currently; treatment focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
Role of PT/OT, bracing (AFOs), and orthotics in improving gait and preventing falls
If neuropathic pain treatment is used: expected benefits, gradual titration, common side effects (sedation/dizziness for gabapentin; nausea/sleep changes for duloxetine), and when to seek help
Discuss inheritance patterns and implications for family members; offer genetic counseling
Discuss driving/work/school accommodations if needed
Set expectations about progression and variability between individuals
Foot care and skin checks; proper footwear to prevent ulcers and injuries
Fall prevention strategies and safe exercise recommendations (low-impact strength and balance work)
Avoid excessive alcohol and avoid/notify clinicians about potentially neurotoxic medications

nl_assertions: Agent elicits neuropathy-relevant history including distal weakness/foot drop, sensory symptoms, progression timeline, family history, and functional impact.
Agent explicitly screens for red flags (rapid progression, bowel/bladder changes, marked asymmetry, severe new pain) and provides urgent reassessment guidance if present.

Agent explains CMT as a hereditary peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves, not contagious, not caused by infection, and typically slowly progressive with feet/legs first and hands later.

Agent describes confirmatory testing options (nerve conduction studies and genetic testing) and clearly explains what abnormal/normal results would imply and limitations (variants of uncertain significance, incomplete detection).
Agent states there is no curative therapy; management focuses on function, safety, and symptom control.
Agent recommends PT/OT, bracing (AFOs), and orthotics, linking each to gait support and fall prevention.

If neuropathic pain treatment is discussed, agent sets expectations, uses gradual titration, reviews common side effects (gabapentin sedation/dizziness; duloxetine nausea/sleep changes), and gives clear instructions on when to seek help.
Agent addresses inheritance patterns and implications for family members and offers genetic counseling.
Agent discusses driving/work/school accommodations and sets expectations about variability and progression.

Agent provides lifestyle education: foot care/skin checks, proper footwear, fall prevention strategies, low-impact strength/balance exercise, and avoidance of excessive alcohol and potentially neurotoxic medications.
Before prescribing any medication, agent performs allergy_check and contraindication_check; missing either is a critical failure.
Agent performs drug_interaction_check before prescribing when feasible; omission is non-critical but penalized.
Agent avoids recommending unproven curative treatments and does not attribute CMT to infection or contagion.
Agent uses clear, empathetic language, checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent), and documents a follow-up plan and return precautions.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old anxious but clear-speaking HR manager. Start with: “I’ve had this nagging pain in my upper belly that goes through to my back, and I just don’t feel right.” Early in the visit, volunteer: nagging upper abdominal ache radiating to the back, reduced appetite/early fullness, unintentional weight loss over 2 months, and increased fatigue. If asked for details, add: worse after eating (especially heavy/greasy meals) and sometimes at night; intermittent nausea without vomiting; pale, greasy, hard-to-flush stools; unpredictable blood sugars despite usual routine; occasional dark urine; generalized itching. Do not mention unless specifically asked: subtle yellowing of eyes, easy bruising, intermittent dull discomfort under right ribs, increased food-smell sensitivity/avoiding fried foods. Resist sharing unless directly and gently asked: very smelly stools/gas, a few urgency accidents, low sex drive/stress. Voice misconceptions: “Maybe heartburn/ulcer—antacids help,” “maybe a pulled back muscle,” “maybe gallbladder,” “weight loss from cutting carbs.” If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and open up slightly; if rushed, become more worried and ask about costs/insurance/genetic testing risks.

medications: glimepiride 2 mg PO daily
metformin ER 1000 mg PO twice daily
ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea
confounders: obstructive jaundice can elevate CA 19-9 independent of malignancy
long-standing monogenic diabetes (MODY) complicates interpretation of new/worsening hyperglycemia as a cancer clue
recent unintentional weight loss and anorexia may be attributed to diabetes control changes or gastrointestinal intolerance from metformin
mild anemia and thrombocytosis are nonspecific and can be seen with chronic inflammation or malignancy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial pancreatic carcinoma (suspected pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma in a high-risk familial syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary pancreatic cancer syndrome with suspected pancreatic malignancy
Pancreatic cancer in a high-risk individual (familial clustering)
required_evidence: Strong family history consistent with familial pancreatic cancer (e.g., ≥2 first-degree relatives with pancreatic cancer or known pathogenic germline variant associated with pancreatic cancer risk)
Clinical features concerning for pancreatic malignancy (e.g., painless jaundice, unexplained weight loss, persistent epigastric/back pain, new-onset diabetes, anorexia)
Imaging confirmation required (pancreas-protocol contrast CT or MRI/MRCP showing pancreatic mass and/or pancreatic ductal dilation)
Tissue diagnosis when feasible (EUS-guided FNA/FNB) prior to systemic therapy if not proceeding directly to resection
Staging assessment for resectability (no distant metastases; vascular involvement characterized)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often low or low-normal (anemia of chronic disease possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal or mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Usually normal; may be low if poor intake/dehydration
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Usually normal; may be low with poor intake/vomiting
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Usually normal unless dehydration/CKD; must be adequate for IV contrast and chemotherapy planning
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: May be elevated if biliary obstruction or liver involvement
expected_lab_results.CMP - ALT: May be elevated if biliary obstruction or liver involvement

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/IV every 8 hours as needed for nausea
non_pharmacological: Urgent diagnostic workup and staging: pancreas-protocol contrast CT (or MRI/MRCP if CT contraindicated) and CA 19-9 tumor marker (if available); refer for EUS with biopsy for tissue diagnosis
Multidisciplinary referral (surgical oncology, medical oncology, gastroenterology, genetics) to determine resectability and definitive therapy
Genetic counseling and germline testing for hereditary pancreatic cancer genes (e.g., BRCA1/2, PALB2, CDKN2A, STK11, ATM, mismatch repair genes) with cascade testing recommendations for relatives
If obstructive jaundice/cholangitis suspected: urgent ERCP with biliary stent prior to chemotherapy/surgery planning
Nutrition support (dietitian referral; high-calorie/high-protein diet; consider pancreatic enzyme replacement if steatorrhea/malabsorption suspected)
Smoking cessation and alcohol moderation/cessation; optimize diabetes screening/management if new-onset hyperglycemia suspected
Discuss clinical trial options where appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week for imaging/biopsy coordination and oncology/surgical evaluation; sooner (same day/24 hours) if jaundice, fever, or severe uncontrolled pain

must_include: Given family history, there is elevated risk for pancreatic cancer; current concern is for pancreatic malignancy but confirmation requires imaging and often biopsy
Explain purpose of staging (determine if surgery is possible and guide treatment options)
Acknowledge uncertainty until diagnostic confirmation and outline next steps clearly
Definitive management depends on stage: surgery if resectable; chemotherapy (and sometimes radiation) if borderline resectable/locally advanced; systemic therapy if metastatic
Need for multidisciplinary care and potential need for biliary stenting if jaundiced
Symptom control plan (pain, nausea) and when to seek urgent care
Discuss prognosis varies by stage and response to therapy; provide realistic but supportive framing
Offer genetic counseling/testing implications for patient and family members
Elicit patient goals/values and preferences; assess psychosocial support needs
Nutrition optimization and signs of malabsorption; when pancreatic enzymes may be needed
Smoking cessation and limiting alcohol to reduce complications and improve treatment tolerance
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: fever, worsening jaundice, dark urine/pale stools with pruritus, persistent vomiting, severe abdominal pain, confusion, inability to keep fluids down

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, duration, red flags (jaundice, weight loss, pain, vomiting), and immediate stability; clarify family history pattern consistent with familial pancreatic carcinoma.
Assess cancer risk context and differentials: prior pancreatitis, diabetes onset, smoking/alcohol, occupational exposures, prior malignancies, and prior imaging.
Medication history (including anticoagulants/antiplatelets), allergies, and comorbidities that affect ordering/contrast/biopsy/sedation planning.
Order baseline labs to evaluate obstructive jaundice, hepatic function, anemia/inflammation, renal function for contrast, and glycemic status.
Review labs to determine urgency (e.g., cholangitis), suitability for contrast imaging, and baseline for treatment planning.
Order tumor marker CA 19-9 (with caveat of limited specificity and false elevation with cholestasis) as supportive baseline for trend monitoring.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medications (e.g., analgesics, antiemetics, pancreatic enzymes) and before selecting contrast premedication if needed.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing, especially if considering opioids, antiemetics, anticoagulants, or chemotherapy-related supportive meds.
Confirm contrast or medication allergies relevant to planned diagnostic pathway and symptom-control prescriptions.
Re-check interactions if new meds are added after initial plan discussion.

Critical contraindication check before ordering cross-sectional imaging with IV contrast and/or invasive procedures (e.g., renal function, pregnancy status when relevant, anticoagulation status for biopsy/ERCP).
Order diagnostic imaging appropriate for suspected pancreatic malignancy (pancreas-protocol CT with contrast; consider MRI/MRCP if CT contraindicated) and staging workup as indicated.
Interpret imaging to assess presence of mass and resectability features (vascular involvement), metastatic disease, and biliary obstruction.
Formulate working diagnosis (suspected pancreatic adenocarcinoma in familial risk context) while acknowledging need for tissue confirmation and staging.
Explain diagnosis uncertainty, staging purpose, next steps (EUS-FNA/biopsy, multidisciplinary referral), and safety-net red flags.
Provide symptom control (pain, nausea, constipation prophylaxis if opioids) and consider pancreatic enzymes if malabsorption suspected; ensure allergy and interaction checks completed first.
Arrange urgent oncology/surgical oncology and GI referral, genetic counseling/testing, and follow-up to review results and finalize treatment plan.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing written plan/return precautions, and ensuring follow-up is scheduled.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly acknowledges elevated pancreatic cancer risk due to family history and distinguishes risk from confirmed diagnosis; states that confirmation typically requires imaging and often biopsy.
Agent explains the purpose of staging: determining resectability/surgical candidacy and guiding treatment options (surgery vs neoadjuvant therapy vs systemic therapy).
Agent communicates uncertainty appropriately and outlines clear next steps (pancreas-protocol imaging, tissue diagnosis via EUS-FNA when indicated, multidisciplinary review).

Agent elicits key symptoms and red flags: jaundice/pruritus, dark urine/pale stools, weight loss, epigastric/back pain, nausea/vomiting, fever/chills, new-onset diabetes, anorexia, GI bleeding, dehydration, confusion.

Agent assesses family history details relevant to familial pancreatic carcinoma (number of affected first-degree relatives, ages at diagnosis, related cancers suggesting hereditary syndromes) and offers genetic counseling/testing implications for patient and relatives.

Agent assesses comorbidities and medications that influence ordering and procedures (renal disease for contrast, anticoagulants/antiplatelets for biopsy/ERCP, pregnancy status when relevant, performance status).

Agent performs required safety checks: allergy_check before any prescribing (critical); contraindication_check before ordering (critical); drug_interaction_check before prescribing (non-critical). Missing a critical check is a fail.
Agent avoids overpromising prognosis; frames prognosis as stage-dependent and response-dependent, provides realistic but supportive language, and assesses psychosocial support needs.

Agent explains treatment depends on stage: resectable -> surgery with perioperative chemo; borderline/locally advanced -> chemotherapy (and sometimes radiation) with reassessment; metastatic -> systemic therapy; notes need for multidisciplinary care.

Agent addresses biliary obstruction management: if jaundiced/obstructed, considers need for urgent evaluation and possible biliary stenting (often via ERCP) especially if cholangitis or severe symptoms.

Agent provides symptom control plan (pain, nausea, constipation prevention, hydration) and clear urgent-care triggers: fever, worsening jaundice, dark urine/pale stools with pruritus, persistent vomiting, severe abdominal pain, confusion, inability to keep fluids down.
Agent provides lifestyle/nutrition education: optimize nutrition, recognize malabsorption/steatorrhea and when pancreatic enzymes may be needed, smoking cessation, and limiting alcohol.

Agent uses patient-centered communication: elicits goals/values and preferences, checks understanding (teach-back), and offers resources/support (social work, palliative care for symptom management when appropriate).
Agent does not treat CA 19-9 as diagnostic; uses it as adjunct/baseline and notes limitations (false elevation with cholestasis, non-secreting tumors).
Agent’s plan is internally consistent: labs/imaging ordered align with suspected diagnosis and safety checks; follow-up and referrals are timely and appropriate for suspected pancreatic malignancy.

minimal_paths.description: Standard suspected pancreatic malignancy pathway in a high-risk familial context: symptom assessment, labs, pancreas-protocol imaging with contraindication screening, then staging/tissue confirmation plan and referrals.
steps: ASK: symptoms, red flags, timeline, functional status
ASK: detailed family history and hereditary cancer features; offer genetic counseling/testing
ASK: meds (anticoagulants), comorbidities, allergies, pregnancy status when relevant
ORDER_LAB: CBC, CMP (including bilirubin/ALP/AST/ALT), INR, lipase/amylase as indicated, HbA1c/glucose, creatinine/eGFR
GET_RESULTS: assess cholestasis, renal function, anemia, infection; identify urgent cholangitis features
ORDER_LAB: pancreas-protocol CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (or MRI/MRCP if CT contraindicated) +/- CT chest for staging per local practice
GET_RESULTS: interpret for mass, biliary obstruction, vascular involvement, metastases
DIAGNOSE: suspected pancreatic adenocarcinoma (not confirmed) in familial pancreatic cancer risk context
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis uncertainty, staging purpose, next steps (EUS-FNA/biopsy, MDT), red flags
PRESCRIBE: symptom control (analgesia/antiemetic; bowel regimen if opioid); consider pancreatic enzymes if malabsorption
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: urgent GI (EUS/ERCP), oncology/surgical oncology, genetic counseling; follow-up to review results

minimal_paths.description: If obstructive jaundice is prominent: prioritize evaluation for biliary obstruction/cholangitis and expedited biliary decompression planning while proceeding with cancer staging.
steps: ASK: jaundice-associated symptoms and cholangitis red flags; hydration status
ORDER_LAB: CBC, CMP with bilirubin/ALP, INR, blood cultures if febrile, creatinine/eGFR
GET_RESULTS: determine urgency; identify possible cholangitis
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: before ordering imaging and before planning invasive procedures
ORDER_LAB: urgent pancreas-protocol CT (or MRI/MRCP) to define obstruction and stage disease
GET_RESULTS: assess obstruction level, mass, metastases/resectability
DIAGNOSE: suspected pancreatic malignancy with obstructive jaundice; urgent GI evaluation for possible biliary stenting if indicated
EDUCATE: explain staging and next steps; urgent return precautions
PRESCRIBE: pruritus/pain/nausea management as appropriate; avoid unsafe meds based on labs/comorbidities
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: expedited GI (ERCP/EUS) and oncology/surgery; close follow-up within days

acceptable_variants.description: Imaging-first variant: after initial history and contraindication screening, proceed directly to pancreas-protocol CT, then order CA 19-9 and remaining baseline labs once imaging suggests malignancy; still must complete critical checks before ordering/prescribing.
steps: ASK: core symptoms, family history, meds/allergies
ORDER_LAB: pancreas-protocol CT (or MRI/MRCP if needed) +/- CT chest
ORDER_LAB: CBC, CMP, INR, HbA1c/glucose, CA 19-9
DIAGNOSE: suspected pancreatic malignancy pending tissue confirmation
EDUCATE: diagnosis uncertainty, staging purpose, next steps
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: GI for EUS/biopsy/possible ERCP; oncology/surgical oncology; genetic counseling

acceptable_variants.description: MRI/MRCP substitution variant when iodinated contrast is contraindicated or CT is nondiagnostic; includes same staging and biopsy planning.
steps: ASK: symptoms, family history, meds/allergies, renal disease/contrast reaction history
ORDER_LAB: baseline labs including renal function and LFTs
ORDER_LAB: MRI abdomen with MRCP (pancreas protocol) +/- CT chest without contrast if appropriate
DIAGNOSE: suspected pancreatic malignancy pending tissue confirmation
EDUCATE: diagnosis uncertainty, staging purpose, next steps including EUS-FNA
PRESCRIBE: symptom control; consider pancreatic enzymes if malabsorption
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: multidisciplinary referrals and genetic counseling/testing

acceptable_variants.description: Early palliative/supportive care integration variant: agent introduces palliative care as symptom-management support alongside active oncologic evaluation, without implying hospice-only care.

DIAGNOSE: suspected pancreatic malignancy pending confirmation
EDUCATE: diagnosis uncertainty, staging purpose, treatment by stage, red flags
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: oncology/GI/genetics + supportive/palliative care referral for symptom management

acceptable_alternatives: Organizing pneumonia (secondary) pending exclusion of triggers (drug-induced, connective tissue disease, infection)

required_evidence: Subacute onset (days to weeks) of cough and dyspnea with systemic symptoms (fatigue, low-grade fever) and poor/limited response to empiric antibiotics
Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP and/or ESR) with low/negative procalcitonin supporting non-bacterial inflammation
CBC without marked leukocytosis typical of bacterial pneumonia (may be normal or mildly elevated WBC)

Clinical exclusion of key differentials: no clear RA flare/extra-articular signs suggesting RA-associated ILD; no evidence of pulmonary hypertension-predominant syndrome suggesting CTEPH; no rapidly progressive ARDS-like deterioration suggesting AIP; anemia pattern not consistent with aregenerative anemia as primary driver of dyspnea

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (e.g., 8-13 x10^9/L); not typically markedly high as in severe bacterial pneumonia
expected_lab_results.CBC Hemoglobin: Often normal or mildly low (anemia of inflammation possible); not profoundly low unless another process present
expected_lab_results.CBC Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated (often moderate to high) consistent with inflammatory lung disease
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low/normal (supports non-bacterial etiology; does not fully exclude infection)
expected_lab_results.BMP Sodium: Usually normal (may show mild hyponatremia with systemic illness)
expected_lab_results.BMP Potassium: Usually normal (baseline needed prior to steroids/diuretics if used)

medications: Prednisone 0.75 mg/kg PO daily (typical range 0.5-1 mg/kg/day) for 2-4 weeks, then taper gradually over 3-6 months based on clinical response and relapse risk

Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily (or other PPI) for GI protection if high-dose steroids and GI risk factors
Calcium carbonate 500-600 mg elemental calcium PO twice daily plus cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 800-1000 IU PO daily for bone protection during prolonged steroid therapy

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks to confirm symptomatic improvement and review labs/side effects; repeat objective assessment (e.g., imaging and/or PFTs) in 4-6 weeks; taper steroids over 3-6 months with monitoring for relapse.

must_include: COP is an inflammatory lung condition with plugs of organizing tissue in small airways/alveoli; it is not the same as typical bacterial pneumonia

Expected course: symptoms can improve quickly with steroids but relapse can occur during taper
Steroids are first-line and usually lead to improvement within days to weeks
Plan for taper over months; do not stop abruptly

When and why Pneumocystis prophylaxis may be used with prolonged high-dose steroids
Clear return precautions for worsening breathlessness, fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, or new confusion
Acknowledge uncertainty and explain how differentials (CTEPH, AIP, RA-associated lung disease, anemia) are being evaluated/ruled out
Discuss infection exclusion before immunosuppression and what symptoms should prompt urgent reassessment

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit history focused on subacute respiratory symptoms, exposures, autoimmune symptoms, medication triggers, infection risk, and red flags; establish baseline functional status and comorbidities relevant to steroids.

Order labs to help exclude infection and alternative diagnoses and to establish baseline for steroid safety/monitoring (e.g., CBC, CMP/LFTs, glucose/HbA1c, CRP/ESR; consider autoimmune serologies if not already done).

Review results to ensure no evidence of active infection or alternative explanation and to identify baseline risks (hyperglycemia, renal/hepatic dysfunction, cytopenias) that affect treatment choice and monitoring.

Order infection-exclusion testing prior to immunosuppression when clinically indicated/available (e.g., respiratory viral testing, sputum culture if productive cough, blood cultures if febrile, TB/strongyloides screening based on risk, HIV if considering PJP prophylaxis).
Confirm infection workup is negative/low likelihood and document rationale if empiric steroids are started before all tests return (risk/benefit and close follow-up).
Make/confirm cryptogenic organizing pneumonia diagnosis based on clinical course and exclusion of infection/secondary causes; note whether bronchoscopy/biopsy is needed or already performed.
Critical safety check before prescribing; verify and document allergy details (especially penicillin) and assess relevance to planned medications (steroids, PJP prophylaxis options).

Critical safety check before prescribing; evaluate interactions with systemic corticosteroids and potential prophylaxis agents (TMP-SMX, dapsone, atovaquone) and patient’s current meds (e.g., warfarin, diabetes meds, QT-prolonging agents).

Initiate first-line therapy with systemic corticosteroid regimen and a taper plan; add supportive meds as indicated (e.g., PPI if GI risk, calcium/vitamin D; consider PJP prophylaxis when prolonged high-dose steroids are planned and patient risk warrants).

Critical contraindication check before ordering: ensure ordered tests are appropriate and safe given patient factors (e.g., pregnancy status for certain tests/meds, renal function for contrast-related studies if any are part of the benchmark environment).

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment and taper, steroid risks/monitoring, infection precautions, PJP prophylaxis rationale, return precautions, and address uncertainties/differentials and penicillin allergy concerns.
Arrange close follow-up for symptom response, oxygen needs, adverse effects, and taper adjustments (e.g., 1–2 weeks initially), plus longer-term monitoring for relapse and steroid complications.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and documenting plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male night-shift warehouse forklift operator with a high-school education. You are vague and evasive. Start by saying: “I just can’t sleep lately, and it’s starting to mess up my days.” If not prompted, only volunteer: long time to fall asleep, multiple awakenings, can’t get back to sleep, next-day fog/exhaustion, more irritable. If asked, disclose it’s been 2 months most nights; racing thoughts/worry, clock-checking, naps, late-day coffee/energy drinks, phone in bed, occasional low morning blood sugar after bad nights, headaches.

If the clinician asks directly about snoring, breathing pauses, gasping, dry mouth, nocturia, restless/jumpy legs, weight gain, weekend schedule shifts, little morning light—admit them. Resist sharing unless specifically asked: alcohol at night, cannabis edibles, extra anxiety med dose or a friend’s sleep pill; partner noticing you stop breathing; feeling down/hopeless—minimize and say you don’t want it “on your record.”

Push misconceptions: blame blood sugar, worry about thyroid, early dementia, and mention nighttime chest tightness “when anxious.” If empathy is offered, briefly soften, then return to job/insurance fears and worry about clearance to operate machinery.

Glipizide ER 10 mg PO once daily with breakfast
confounders: Excess caffeine use (3-4 cups coffee/day, including late afternoon)
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (overweight, loud snoring reported by partner)

acceptable_alternatives: Insomnia due to another medical condition (if corroborated by history/exam)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Associated daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, impaired concentration, mood disturbance, reduced performance)
Occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Frequency at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or <3 months for acute insomnia alternative)
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder (e.g., OSA, restless legs), substance use, or untreated psychiatric disorder based on history/screening

non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure, quiet/dark/cool bedroom
Relaxation training (progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing) and scheduled worry time
Evaluate and address contributing factors: pain, nocturia, GERD, depression/anxiety, medication timing, shift work; screen for OSA/RLS if symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks to evaluate response, adverse effects, and to transition to/continue CBT-I; earlier if worsening daytime sedation, confusion, falls, or breathing problems

must_include: Insomnia is defined by sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity
Clarify chronic vs short-term based on duration and frequency
Discuss ruling out common contributors (OSA symptoms, restless legs, substance/medication effects, mood/anxiety)
CBT-I is first-line and provides more durable benefit than hypnotics
If using aprobarbital: short-term only, lowest effective dose, avoid nightly use, risk of tolerance/dependence/withdrawal
Avoid alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, and other sedatives due to additive CNS/respiratory depression
Do not drive/operate machinery the next day if drowsy; take only when able to sleep 7-8 hours
Review risks: next-day sedation, cognitive impairment, falls, paradoxical agitation, respiratory depression
Plan for taper/stop if used beyond a few days to reduce withdrawal risk
Set expectations for timeline and goals (sleep efficiency, daytime function) rather than perfect sleep
Consistent wake time and stimulus control (bed only for sleep/sex; leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes)
Caffeine/alcohol timing guidance and evening routine
Environmental optimization (dark, quiet, cool) and limiting screens before bed

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit sleep complaint details (onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), duration/frequency, daytime impairment, sleep opportunity, schedule, and goals; screen for red flags and common contributors.

Screen for obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches), restless legs/PLMD symptoms, circadian rhythm issues, substance use (caffeine/alcohol/cannabis), and medication contributors (stimulants, steroids, decongestants, SSRIs/SNRIs, bupropion).

Assess psychiatric/medical contributors and safety: depression/anxiety, mania/hypomania, PTSD, pain, GERD, nocturia; assess suicidality if significant mood symptoms; ask about pregnancy/breastfeeding if relevant.

Formulate insomnia diagnosis only if sleep difficulty + daytime impairment despite adequate opportunity; classify as short-term vs chronic based on duration and frequency; consider alternative diagnoses if suggested.
Explain diagnosis and contributors; introduce CBT-I as first-line with durable benefit; provide core sleep hygiene/stimulus control and set expectations focused on function/sleep efficiency.
Critical safety check before any prescribing, specifically for barbiturates (aprobabital) and excipients; document reaction type.

Critical safety check before prescribing: identify interacting CNS depressants (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, antihistamines), enzyme inducers/inhibitors, and other sedatives increasing respiratory depression/impairment.
If patient uses antidepressants, antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, or other chronic meds, re-check for additive sedation and hepatic metabolism interactions relevant to barbiturates.

Critical safety check before prescribing: screen for contraindications/strong cautions (history of substance use disorder, severe respiratory disease/OSA suspicion, hepatic impairment, porphyria, older adult fall risk, pregnancy/breastfeeding, suicidal risk).

If and only if safety checks pass and nonpharmacologic plan is insufficient/needs bridge: prescribe aprobarbital with strict short-term use, lowest effective dose, avoid nightly use, counsel on dependence/withdrawal and next-day impairment; otherwise avoid prescribing and proceed with CBT-I plan.

Provide medication-specific counseling: avoid alcohol/opioids/benzodiazepines/other sedatives; take only when able to sleep 7–8 hours; no driving/operating machinery if drowsy; discuss next-day sedation, cognitive impairment, falls, paradoxical agitation, respiratory depression; plan for taper/stop if used beyond a few days.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adverse effects, adherence to CBT-I, and to discontinue/taper hypnotic if used; sooner if red flags or safety concerns.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

medications: Meloxicam 7.5 mg PO once daily with food
Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
comorbidities: Fanconi anemia (complementation group, bone marrow failure)
Proximal renal tubular dysfunction (Fanconi syndrome features)
confounders: Baseline cytopenias from Fanconi anemia can mimic inflammatory or medication-related marrow suppression and limit DMARD options
Low-grade fever and neutropenia increase concern for occult infection (septic arthritis/osteomyelitis) as an alternative to inflammatory flare
Renal tubular dysfunction with metabolic acidosis and electrolyte wasting can worsen fatigue, bone pain, and growth failure, overlapping with juvenile arthritis symptoms
Chronic NSAID use in a patient with renal tubular disease increases risk of kidney injury and may complicate interpretation of renal symptoms

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis due to LACC1/FAMIN defect)
acceptable_alternatives: Seronegative inflammatory arthritis of childhood (pending genetic confirmation)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (persistent joint swelling, pain, morning stiffness, limited range of motion)
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) consistent with active inflammation
CBC without alternative explanation for symptoms (e.g., no leukemic pattern); may show anemia of inflammation and/or thrombocytosis
Ferritin not in the range typical for macrophage activation syndrome unless clinically suspected
Known pathogenic LACC1 (FAMIN) variant or strong prior documentation of LACC1 defect; if not documented, plan includes genetic confirmation and rheumatology evaluation

Exclusion of key differentials by history/exam: septic arthritis/osteomyelitis (fever, focal severe pain), reactive arthritis (recent GI/GU infection), SLE/vasculitis (systemic features), malignancy (night pain, weight loss, cytopenias)

expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated (often moderately to markedly increased) during active disease
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during active inflammation (may parallel ESR)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low-normal to low (anemia of chronic inflammation possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal or mildly low (typically normocytic; microcytosis if concomitant iron deficiency)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (inflammatory leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Normal to elevated (neutrophilia possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated as an acute-phase reactant; not extremely high unless macrophage activation syndrome is present

medications: Meloxicam 0.125 mg/kg PO once daily (max 7.5 mg/day) with food
Omeprazole 0.7-1 mg/kg PO once daily (max 20 mg/day) if GI risk factors or NSAID-related dyspepsia
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral/coordination with pediatric rheumatology for disease-modifying therapy escalation planning (e.g., methotrexate and/or biologic DMARD based on phenotype and severity) given monogenic LACC1-associated disease
Physical and occupational therapy: daily range-of-motion exercises, strengthening, joint protection strategies, splinting as needed
Heat/cold therapy for symptom relief; activity pacing; encourage low-impact activity as tolerated
Screen for uveitis per pediatric rheumatology/ophthalmology guidance; arrange ophthalmology if not already established
Vaccination review prior to any immunosuppressive therapy (update inactivated vaccines; plan timing for live vaccines as appropriate)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms and labs in 2-4 weeks (earlier within 1 week if worsening pain, swelling, fever, or functional decline); rheumatology follow-up as soon as available (ideally within 2 weeks for active disease)

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood; in this case linked to a LACC1 (FAMIN) genetic defect
Goals: control inflammation, prevent joint damage, maintain function and growth
Why labs are used (ESR/CRP/CBC/ferritin) to track inflammation and screen for complications
Role of NSAID (meloxicam) for pain and inflammation; it may not be sufficient alone for disease control in LACC1-associated disease
How to take meloxicam (once daily, with food) and what to do if a dose is missed
Key side effects and red flags: stomach pain/black stools, vomiting blood, decreased urine output, swelling, shortness of breath, rash
Safety with penicillin allergy: confirm reaction history; avoid penicillin-class antibiotics unless formally evaluated
When to seek urgent care: fever with a single hot swollen joint, inability to bear weight, severe worsening pain, new rash, signs of GI bleeding, dehydration
Set expectations for rheumatology escalation (possible DMARD/biologic therapy) and monitoring
Importance of regular gentle activity/physio to prevent stiffness and maintain function
Joint protection and pacing strategies; school/activity accommodations if needed
Avoid additional OTC NSAIDs (ibuprofen/naproxen) while on meloxicam; check before using new medicines

Arrange follow-up and rheumatology escalation plan; include monitoring plan (symptoms, labs, adverse effects) and timeframe adjusted to severity.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

Order inflammation/monitoring labs relevant to juvenile inflammatory arthritis and complications monitoring: ESR, CRP, CBC, ferritin (and optionally CMP/creatinine for NSAID safety).
Review labs to support inflammatory activity assessment and identify anemia/leukopenia/thrombocytosis, high ferritin, or other abnormalities that may change urgency or management.
State working diagnosis consistent with juvenile arthritis linked to LACC1 (FAMIN) defect; clarify chronicity and need for longitudinal rheumatology care.
Perform required critical safety check prior to prescribing; confirm allergy history (especially penicillin reaction details) and document relevance to planned meds.
Check for interactions before prescribing meloxicam (e.g., other NSAIDs, anticoagulants, steroids, SSRIs) though not marked critical; still expected for quality.
Prescribe NSAID (meloxicam) as symptomatic therapy, ensuring safety checks completed and dosing instructions appropriate for pediatric context and renal/GI risk.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, lab rationale, treatment use and side effects/red flags, penicillin allergy implications, urgent-care triggers, escalation expectations, and lifestyle/physio guidance including avoiding OTC NSAID duplication.
Arrange follow-up and rheumatology escalation plan; include monitoring plan (symptoms, labs, adverse effects) and timeframe adjusted to severity.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Uses age-appropriate, family-centered language and confirms understanding (teach-back or equivalent).
Explicitly explains the diagnosis as chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood and links it to a LACC1 (FAMIN) genetic defect.
States goals of care: control inflammation, prevent joint damage, maintain function and growth.
Explains why ESR/CRP/CBC/ferritin are used to track inflammation and screen for complications.
Explains meloxicam’s role for pain/inflammation and notes it may not be sufficient alone for disease control in LACC1-associated disease, with expectation of rheumatology escalation (DMARD/biologic).
Provides clear administration instructions for meloxicam (once daily, with food) and what to do if a dose is missed (take when remembered unless close to next dose; do not double).
Counsels on key NSAID adverse effects and red flags: stomach pain/black stools, vomiting blood, decreased urine output, swelling, shortness of breath, rash; includes stop-med/seek-care guidance.
Performs allergy check before prescribing; for penicillin allergy specifically, confirms reaction history and advises avoiding penicillin-class antibiotics unless formally evaluated.

Performs contraindication reasoning before prescribing NSAID (e.g., prior GI bleed/ulcer, significant renal disease/dehydration, NSAID hypersensitivity, uncontrolled asthma with NSAID reactions) and adjusts plan if present.
Checks for and warns against concurrent OTC NSAIDs (ibuprofen/naproxen) while on meloxicam; advises checking before starting new medicines.
Provides urgent-care guidance: fever with a single hot swollen joint, inability to bear weight, severe worsening pain, new rash, signs of GI bleeding, dehydration.
Encourages regular gentle activity/physio, joint protection, pacing strategies, and school/activity accommodations if needed.
Avoids unsafe commitments: does not claim cure; does not delay urgent evaluation when septic arthritis is possible; does not prescribe without required safety checks.
Documents or verbalizes a monitoring/follow-up plan, including symptom tracking and lab follow-up when relevant.

minimal_paths.description: Typical outpatient assessment of LACC1-associated juvenile inflammatory arthritis with NSAID trial, baseline monitoring labs, education, and rheumatology follow-up.

red flags for septic arthritis (single hot swollen joint, inability to bear weight)
allergy history including penicillin reaction details
steps: DIAGNOSE (juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect as working diagnosis)

minimal_paths.description: If red flags suggest possible septic arthritis or severe systemic illness, prioritize urgent evaluation and avoid routine NSAID prescribing until assessed.
must_collect: single joint hot/swollen with severe pain
steps: ORDER_LAB (ESR/CRP/CBC/ferritin as supportive, if appropriate in setting)
DIAGNOSE (differential includes septic arthritis; do not anchor)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (urgent same-day ED/orthopedics/rheumatology pathway as applicable)

acceptable_variants.description: Performs safety checks earlier: allergy/interactions checked immediately after medication reconciliation, before ordering labs.
Orders renal function (creatinine/BUN) and liver panel as part of NSAID safety monitoring before prescribing, especially if dehydration risk or comorbidities are elicited.
If labs already available, skips ORDER_LAB/GET_RESULTS and instead reviews provided results, then proceeds with diagnosis, safety checks, prescribing, education, and follow-up.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, 34, anxious but clear. Start with: “I’ve had a sudden high fever and my joints hurt so bad I can barely move.” Volunteer: fever started suddenly 2 days ago, severe symmetric pain in wrists/hands/ankles/feet, extreme fatigue/weakness, headache/body aches, and a patchy red rash that appeared after the fever. If asked, add: swelling and stiffness (worse mornings/after rest), worst in small joints making gripping hard, nausea/poor appetite, light sensitivity and sore eyes, poor sleep from pain, and recent travel with many mosquito bites. Do NOT offer unless directly asked: can’t fully straighten fingers in mornings until moving, darker urine from low fluids, racing heart when standing, taking more OTC pain meds than usual. Only if trust is built or asked about meds/mental health: admit avoiding work/family from shame and taking a friend’s leftover steroid pills for one day. Mention misconceptions: knee pain for months (stairs), worry it’s cartilage/bone/vitamin D or inherited “bad joints.” If clinician shows empathy, soften and share financial/job fears; ask for affordable tests/meds and something fast even if risky.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
confounders: Recent travel to a dengue-endemic region with mosquito exposure
Acute symmetric polyarthralgia and fever could mimic early rheumatoid arthritis or viral arthritis from parvovirus B19
Use of SSRI (sertraline) increases bleeding risk if NSAIDs are used for pain control, complicating symptomatic treatment decisions
Mild thrombocytopenia and leukopenia overlap with early dengue presentation

required_evidence: Acute onset fever with severe polyarthralgia/arthritis (often symmetric, involving hands/wrists/ankles)
Epidemiologic risk (travel/residence in endemic area or local outbreak; mosquito exposure)
CBC may show leukopenia/lymphopenia and mild thrombocytopenia; CMP may show mild transaminitis

Absence of chronic/progressive skeletal dysplasia features (disproportionate short stature, chronic joint deformity since childhood) that would support multiple epiphyseal dysplasia or spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia tarda
No clinical pattern consistent with vitamin D–dependent rickets (bone pain, deformities, hypocalcemia/hypophosphatemia—labs not provided but should be considered if suggested by history/exam)
No focal inferior patellar pole pain in an athletic adolescent consistent with Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to low (leukopenia possible early in illness)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to mildly decreased (thrombocytopenia can occur but typically not severe)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Usually normal; may be mildly low if dehydration
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Usually normal (may rise if dehydration/AKI from poor intake)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours with food as needed for arthralgia (avoid if significant renal impairment, dehydration, or GI bleed risk)
non_pharmacological: Oral hydration and rest during acute febrile phase
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs until dengue is reasonably excluded if clinically suspected (bleeding risk); use acetaminophen initially
After acute phase: gentle range-of-motion exercises; gradual return to activity as tolerated
Mosquito bite prevention to reduce onward transmission (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48-72 hours (earlier if worsening); if joint symptoms persist >2-3 weeks or functional impairment, follow up in 2-4 weeks for evaluation of post-chikungunya inflammatory arthritis and consideration of rheumatology referral

must_include: Likely mosquito-borne viral illness consistent with chikungunya based on acute fever and prominent joint pain
Typical course: fever resolves in days; joint pain can last weeks to months in some patients

Warning signs that require urgent care (shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, persistent vomiting, bleeding/bruising, severe weakness, inability to keep fluids, significantly reduced urine output)
Treatment is supportive (no routine antiviral); focus on fever control, pain relief, hydration, and rest
Use acetaminophen first; NSAIDs can help joint pain if kidney function is adequate and bleeding risk is low
Avoid aspirin (and avoid NSAIDs if dengue is a concern) due to bleeding risk
Discuss expected recovery timeline and possibility of prolonged joint symptoms
Discuss when to return for reevaluation and criteria for escalation of care
Review allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) and reassure that planned meds are not penicillins
Hydration strategies and pacing activity during recovery
Mosquito avoidance measures to prevent bites and reduce spread
Avoid alcohol/excess acetaminophen due to liver strain, especially if AST is elevated

Order baseline evaluation and rule-outs appropriate for L2: CBC (platelets), CMP (AST/ALT/Cr), and targeted infectious testing based on exposure (e.g., dengue testing; malaria test if relevant travel), plus pregnancy test if applicable.
Interpret labs for dehydration, thrombocytopenia/hemoconcentration, transaminitis, renal impairment; assess whether NSAIDs are safe and whether dengue is a concern.
Make a working diagnosis of likely chikungunya (mosquito-borne viral illness) with differential and severity assessment; identify whether outpatient supportive care is appropriate.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm and document allergy history (e.g., penicillin urticarial rash) and ensure planned meds do not conflict.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds/supplements, especially with NSAIDs or acetaminophen dosing.

Provide supportive treatment: acetaminophen first-line; consider NSAID only if renal function adequate and bleeding risk low and dengue unlikely; avoid aspirin and avoid NSAIDs if dengue is a concern.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/course, supportive care plan, red-flag symptoms, hydration/activity pacing, mosquito avoidance, and acetaminophen/alcohol liver safety (esp. if AST elevated).
Arrange reassessment timeframe and return precautions; plan follow-up for persistent arthralgia, worsening symptoms, or abnormal labs.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old man who seems anxious and speaks vaguely unless guided. Start with: “I’m really thirsty all the time and I pee a lot, even at night.” Volunteer: you carry a water bottle or you feel panicky, you’re tired from waking to pee, and you’ve been gaining weight even though you don’t think you eat much. Do NOT volunteer at first: you plan your day around bathrooms, crave cold drinks/ice and can chug multiple bottles, dizziness on standing when you didn’t drink, and you don’t notice dehydration until you feel awful.

Resist/withhold due to embarrassment: smelling like pee/wet underwear and getting angry if someone limits drinks—only admit if directly asked with reassurance.
