medications: sertraline 50 mg PO daily
risperidone 0.5 mg PO twice daily
melatonin 3 mg PO nightly as needed
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: Limited ability to describe symptoms reliably due to intellectual disability
Behavioral dysregulation and anxiety may mimic or mask pain/medical illness
Psychotropic-associated weight gain and dyslipidemia (risperidone) complicating metabolic assessment
Possible obstructive sleep apnea suggested by obesity and sleep disturbance, contributing to inattention/irritability
Mild CK elevation may be from increased activity/restraint episodes or antipsychotic-related muscle effects rather than primary neuromuscular disease

differentials: developmental delay, intellectual disability, obesity, and dysmorphic features

acceptable_alternatives: X-linked syndromic neurodevelopmental disorder (pending genetic confirmation)
Intellectual developmental disorder due to suspected X-linked genetic syndrome
required_evidence: Documented deficits in intellectual functioning (standardized testing or equivalent clinical assessment) and adaptive functioning with onset in the developmental period
Family history suggesting X-linked inheritance (multiple affected males, carrier females possibly mildly affected)
Syndromic features if present (e.g., dysmorphic features, obesity, neurobehavioral symptoms) to support a genetic syndrome
Exclusion/assessment of mimics or contributors listed in differentials (e.g., evaluate polyuria/polydipsia for central diabetes insipidus; assess migraine phenotype; screen for anxiety disorder)
Plan for confirmatory genetic evaluation (chromosomal microarray ± Fragile X testing ± targeted gene panel/exome based on phenotype and family history)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no specific expected abnormality attributable to X-linked intellectual disability)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Sodium: Typically normal; if central diabetes insipidus is present/active, may be high-normal to elevated (hypernatremia) depending on access to water

medications: No disease-specific pharmacotherapy for intellectual disability itself; treat comorbidities if diagnosed (e.g., anxiety) per standard guidelines
non_pharmacological: Initiate/continue individualized education plan (IEP) or adult disability supports; formal neuropsychological and adaptive functioning assessment if not already done
Genetic counseling and referral to clinical genetics for diagnostic confirmation (CMA, Fragile X, and/or gene panel/exome guided by phenotype and family history)

Screen and manage comorbidities: anxiety (validated screening tools; psychotherapy/CBT adapted for cognitive level), obesity (nutrition/physical activity plan; assess for sleep apnea), migraine history (trigger management; headache diary; neurology referral if familial hemiplegic migraine suspected)

Assess for symptoms of diabetes insipidus (polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia); if present, refer for endocrine evaluation (serum/urine osmolality, water deprivation test under supervision) before any desmopressin use
Speech/language therapy, occupational therapy, and social skills/supportive services as indicated
Safety and capacity assessment; discuss guardianship/supported decision-making as appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to review referrals, functional supports, and screening results; earlier (1-2 weeks) if concerning symptoms such as significant polyuria/polydipsia, weight change, severe headaches/neurologic deficits, or escalating anxiety

must_include: Intellectual disability is defined by difficulties in intellectual and adaptive functioning starting in childhood/developmental period
X-linked means a genetic cause on the X chromosome; this can affect males more severely and has implications for family members
Diagnosis is clinical plus confirmatory testing; genetic testing can clarify cause, prognosis, and recurrence risk
There is no single medication that treats intellectual disability itself; care focuses on supports, therapies, and treating associated conditions
Outline referrals (genetics, neuropsychology, therapy services; neurology/endocrinology if indicated) and what each will help with
Set expectations for follow-up and how results will change management
Discuss inheritance/recurrence risk and offer genetic counseling for patient/family
Review red-flag symptoms needing urgent care (new focal neurologic deficits, severe sudden headache, inability to maintain hydration, confusion/lethargy, signs of severe hypernatremia)
Confirm and document allergies (penicillin urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and how they affect care
If obesity is present: practical nutrition and activity goals tailored to cognitive abilities and caregiver supports

Stress/anxiety management strategies and caregiver education; encourage consistent routines and behavioral supports

Order confirmatory/etiologic evaluation as indicated (e.g., chromosomal microarray and/or targeted gene panel/exome per presentation; consider Fragile X testing; basic medical screening labs tailored to symptoms such as TSH, CMP, CBC; additional tests if seizures/endocrine features suspected).
Review results to support clinical diagnosis, identify an X-linked genetic etiology when present, and guide prognosis/recurrence counseling and referrals.
State the diagnosis (intellectual disability with suspected/confirmed X-linked etiology) and document severity based on adaptive functioning; note associated conditions if identified.

Explain treatment: no medication treats intellectual disability itself; focus on supports/therapies, management of comorbidities, and referrals (genetics, neuropsychology, speech/OT/PT, behavioral therapy; neurology/endocrinology if indicated). Set expectations for follow-up and how results change management.

Address concerns: inheritance/recurrence risk and offer genetic counseling; review red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care; confirm/document allergies (penicillin urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and implications for care.

Lifestyle education tailored to cognitive abilities/caregiver supports: if obesity present set practical nutrition/activity goals; sleep hygiene and screen for sleep apnea if snoring/daytime sleepiness; stress/anxiety management, caregiver education, consistent routines/behavioral supports.
Arrange follow-up to review testing, coordinate referrals, update care plan, and monitor comorbidities and functional goals.
Required before any prescribing: verify allergies and reaction types (penicillin urticaria; latex contact dermatitis) and document clearly.
If prescribing any medication for comorbidities, check for drug-drug interactions (non-critical but expected when applicable).
Only if treating associated conditions (e.g., seizures, anxiety, ADHD, sleep issues) with guideline-concordant therapy; must occur after allergy and contraindication checks.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused headache history and neurologic symptom timeline to support familial hemiplegic migraine (FHM) vs stroke/TIA and to assess severity, frequency, disability, triggers, and medication use.

Clarify past diagnoses, family history of hemiplegic migraine, prior workup (imaging/neurology), current meds/supplements, pregnancy status if relevant, and comorbidities affecting treatment choice (e.g., hypotension, bradycardia, constipation, asthma).
Confirm reported allergies/intolerances and reaction types before selecting antiemetic and any other prescriptions; critical safety requirement.
Assess red flags and need for urgent evaluation: first/worst headache, prolonged weakness, new pattern, persistent neuro deficits, fever/neck stiffness, seizure, head trauma, anticoagulant use.

State working diagnosis (familial hemiplegic migraine) with differential (stroke/TIA, seizure, structural lesion) and rationale based on episodic reversible weakness with migraine features and family history.
Optional safety check: screen for interactions with planned acute meds (NSAIDs/acetaminophen, antiemetic) and preventive verapamil (e.g., beta-blockers, digoxin, CYP3A4 inhibitors).
Provide acute and preventive regimen consistent with constraints: acute NSAID/analgesic early; anti-nausea option not metoclopramide; initiate verapamil with gradual titration and monitoring.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis (including stroke mimic and genetic implications), explain treatment (including medication overuse limits and verapamil monitoring), address concerns (allergies, red flags, diabetes insipidus reassurance), and lifestyle education (sleep, hydration, meals, diary, weight/activity).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, titrate preventive therapy, monitor vitals/side effects, and consider neurology referral/genetic counseling/testing discussion.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-net, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent distinguishes FHM from stroke/TIA by explicitly assessing time course of weakness, reversibility, associated migraine features, and red flags, and recommends urgent evaluation for new, unusually severe, or prolonged deficits.

Agent completes an allergy check before prescribing and explicitly avoids metoclopramide due to prior severe akathisia and avoids TMP-SMX due to sulfonamide allergy; agent asks what reactions occurred.

Agent performs a contraindication screen before prescribing verapamil (e.g., baseline bradycardia, hypotension, heart block, heart failure with reduced EF, concurrent beta-blocker use) and documents the rationale for safety.

Agent proposes an acute plan using early analgesic/NSAID and offers an antiemetic option that is not metoclopramide (e.g., ondansetron or prochlorperazine if appropriate), and cautions against medication overuse (no more than 2–3 days/week on average).

Agent proposes a preventive plan with daily verapamil, gradual titration, and monitoring for low blood pressure, slow heart rate, constipation, and dizziness; advises checking pulse/BP and when to seek care.
Agent explains that hemiplegic migraine can cause temporary one-sided weakness as part of aura and typically fully resolves, using clear, non-alarming language.
Agent addresses genetic/familial nature: offers discussion of genetic testing, inheritance implications, and advising relatives to seek evaluation if symptomatic; includes shared decision-making.

Agent provides safety-net instructions with specific red flags and clear ED/911 thresholds (persistent weakness, new speech/vision changes, severe sudden headache, seizure, confusion, fever/neck stiffness).

Agent addresses patient concern about diabetes insipidus by explaining why current labs do not suggest it (no hypernatremia/dehydration pattern) and states what symptoms would prompt further evaluation (polyuria/polydipsia, nocturia, high urine volume).

Agent delivers lifestyle counseling: hydration, sleep regularity, consistent meals, trigger identification via headache diary, and weight management/activity as tolerated, linking obesity to headache burden without stigmatizing language.

Agent avoids contraindicated acute migraine vasoconstrictors in hemiplegic migraine (e.g., triptans/ergots) or, if mentioned, clearly states they are generally avoided and defers to neurology guidance.
Agent’s plan is internally consistent: dosing/titration, monitoring, and follow-up timing align with the stated diagnosis and safety checks.

minimal_paths.description: Focused assessment confirms typical recurrent reversible hemiplegic aura with migraine features and family history; provides safe acute and preventive plan with required counseling and safety-net.
must_collect: onset and duration of unilateral weakness
reversibility of neurologic symptoms and return to baseline
aura features (visual/sensory/speech) and sequence relative to headache

red flags (first/worst, persistent deficit, fever/neck stiffness, seizure, trauma)

steps: ASK: characterize episodes, weakness timeline, migraine features, triggers, frequency, and medication use
ASK: screen red flags and need for urgent evaluation
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm TMP-SMX and metoclopramide reactions; document
DIAGNOSE: familial hemiplegic migraine; note stroke mimic and differential

acceptable_variants.description: Orders baseline evaluation prior to verapamil initiation (e.g., ECG) and/or checks vitals before prescribing, while still completing required counseling and allergy/contraindication checks.

Uses a different non-metoclopramide antiemetic option (e.g., ondansetron) and provides nonpharmacologic acute measures (dark room, hydration) while maintaining medication-overuse limits and verapamil monitoring.
Adds neurology referral/genetic counseling discussion within education/follow-up without changing core medication plan.
